html:
XXVII

Volochine owned immense works in St.┬аPetersburg upon which the existence of thousands of his employees depended.

At the present time, while a strike was in progress, he had turned his back upon the crowd of hungry, dirty malcontents, and was enjoying a trip in the provinces. Libertine as he was, he thought of nothing but women, and in young, fresh, provincial women he displayed an intense, in fact, an absorbing interest. He pictured them as delightfully shy and timid, yet sturdy as a woodland mushroom, and their provocative perfume of youth and purity he scented from afar.

Volochine had clothed his puny little body in virgin white, after sprinkling himself from head to foot with various essences; and, although he did not exactly approve of SarudineтАЩs society, he hailed a droschky and hastened to the latterтАЩs rooms.

Sarudine was sitting at the window, drinking cold tea.

тАЬWhat a lovely evening!тАЭ he kept saying to himself, as he looked out on the garden. But his thoughts were elsewhere. He felt ashamed and afraid.

He was afraid of Lida. Since their interview, he had not set eyes on her. To him she seemed another Lida now, unlike the one that had surrendered to his passion.

тАЬAnyhow,тАЭ he thought, тАЬthe matter is not at an end yet. The child must be got rid ofтБатАКтБатАж or shall I treat the whole thing as a joke? I wonder what she is doing now?тАЭ

He seemed to see before him LidaтАЩs handsome, inscrutable eyes, and her lips tightly compressed, vindictive, menacing.

тАЬShe may be going to pay me out? A girl of that sort isnтАЩt one to be trifled with. At all costs I shall have toтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

The prospect of a huge scandal vaguely suggested itself, striking terror to his craven heart.

тАЬAfter all,тАЭ he thought, тАЬwhat could she possibly do?тАЭ Then suddenly it all seemed quite clear and simple. тАЬPerhaps sheтАЩll drown herself? Let her go to the deuce! I didnтАЩt force her to do it! TheyтАЩll say that she was my mistressтБатАФwell, what of that? It only proves that I am a good-looking fellow. I never said that I would marry her. Upon my word, itтАЩs too silly!тАЭ Sarudine shrugged his shoulders, yet the sense of oppression was not lessened. тАЬPeople will talk, I expect, and I shanтАЩt be able to show myself,тАЭ he thought, while his hand trembled slightly as he held the glass of cold, over-sweetened tea to his lips.

He was as smart and well-groomed and scented as ever, yet it seemed as if, on his face, his white jacket, and his hands, and even on his heart, there was a foul stain which became even greater.

тАЬBah! After a while it will all blow over. And itтАЩs not the first time, either!тАЭ Thus he sought to soothe his conscience, but an inward voice refused to accept such consolation.

Volochine entered gingerly, his boots creaking loudly, and his discoloured teeth revealed by a condescending smile. The room was instantly filled with an odour of musk and of tobacco, quite overpowering the fresh scents of the garden.

тАЬAh! how do you do, Pavel Lvovitsch!тАЭ cried Sarudine as he hastily rose.

Volochine shook hands, sat down by the window and proceeded to light a cigar. He looked so elegant and self-possessed, that Sarudine felt somewhat envious, and endeavoured to assume an equally careless demeanour; but ever since Lida had flung the word тАЬbruteтАЭ in his face, he had felt ill at ease, as if everyone had heard the insult and was secretly mocking him.

Volochine smiled, and chatted about various trifling matters. Yet he found it difficult to keep up such superficial conversation. тАЬWomanтАЭ was the theme that he longed to approach, and it underlay all his stale jokes and stories of the strike at his St.┬аPetersburg factory.

As he lighted another cigar he took the opportunity of looking hard a Sarudine. Their eyes met, and they instantly understood each other. Volochine adjusted his pince-nez and smiled a smile that found it reflection In SarudineтАЩs face which suddenly acquired a look of lust.

тАЬI donтАЩt expect you waste much of your time, do you?тАЭ said Volochine, with a knowing wink.

тАЬOh! as for that, well, what else is there to do?тАЭ replied Sarudine, shrugging his shoulders slightly.

Then they both laughed, and for a while were silent. Volochine was eager to have details of the otherтАЩs conquests. A little vein just below his left knee throbbed convulsively. Sarudine, however, was not thinking of such piquant details, but of the distressing events of the last few days. He turned towards the garden and drummed with his fingers on the windowsill.

Yet Volochine was evidently waiting, and Sarudine felt that he must keep to the desired theme of conversation.

тАЬOf course, I know,тАЭ he began, with an exaggerated air of nonchalance, тАЬI know that to you men-about-town these country wenches are extraordinarily attractive. But youтАЩre wrong. TheyтАЩre fresh and plump, itтАЩs true, but theyтАЩve no chic; they donтАЩt know how to make love artistically.тАЭ

In a moment Volochine was all animation. His eyes sparkled, and there was a change in the tone of his voice.

тАЬNo, thatтАЩs quite true. But after a while all that sort of thing is apt to become boring. Our Petersburg women are not well made. You know what I mean? TheyтАЩre just bundles of nerves; theyтАЩve no limbs on them. Now hereтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬYes, youтАЩre right,тАЭ said Sarudine, growing interested in his turn, as he twirled his moustache complacently.

тАЬTake off her corset, and the smartest Petersburg woman becomesтБатАФOh! by the way, have you heard the latest?тАЭ said Volochine, interrupting himself.

тАЬNo, I dare say not,тАЭ replied Sarudine, leaning forward, eagerly.

тАЬWell,тАЭ said the other, тАЬitтАЩs an awfully good story about a Parisian cocotte.тАЭ Then, with much wealth of detail, Volochine proceeded to relate a spicy anecdote that pleased his companion vastly.

тАЬYes,тАЭ said Volochine in conclusion, as he rolled his eyes, тАЬshapeтАЩs everything in a woman. If she hasnтАЩt got that, well, for me she simply doesnтАЩt exist.тАЭ

Sarudine thought of LidaтАЩs beauty, and he shrank from discussing it with Volochine. However, after a pause, he observed with much affectation:

тАЬEveryone to his taste. What I like most in a woman is the back; that sinuous line, donтАЩt you know.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬYes,тАЭ drawled Volochine nervously.

тАЬSome women, especially very young ones, have gotтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

The orderly now entered treading clumsily in his heavy boots. He had come to light the lamp, and during the process of striking matches and jingling the glass shade, Sarudine and Volochine were silent.

As the flame of the lamp rose, only their glittering eyes and the glowing cigarette-ends could be seen. When the soldier had gone out, they returned to their subject, the word тАЬWomanтАЭ forming the theme of talk that became at times grotesque in its obscenity. SarudineтАЩs instinctive longing to boast, and to eclipse Volochine led him at last to speak of the splendid woman who had yielded to his charms, and gradually to reveal his own secret lasciviousness. Before the eyes of Volochine, Lida was exhibited as in a state of nudity, her physical attributes and her passion all being displayed as though she were some animal for sale at a fair. By their filthy thoughts she was touched and polluted and held up to ridicule. Their love of woman knew no gratitude for the enjoyment given to them; they merely strove to humiliate and insult the sex, to inflict upon it indescribable pain.

The smoke-laden atmosphere of the room had become stifling. Their bodies at fever heat, exhaled an unwholesome odour, as their eyes gleamed and their voices sounded shrill and rabid as those of wild beasts.

Beyond the window lay the calm, clear moonlit night. But for them the world with all its wealth of colour and sound had vanished; all that their eyes beheld was a vision of woman in her nude loveliness. Soon their imagination became so heated that they felt a burning desire to see Lida, whom now they had dubbed Lidka, by way of being familiar. Sarudine had the horses harnessed, and they drove to a house situated on the outskirts of the town.
